prompt: Which dharmaśāstra digest opens by tracing its patron's dynasty back to a wrestler-king named Medinīmalla of the Sīkāśīrāja lineage—'a wrestler upon the earth with the weight of his mighty forces' whose 'fame [was] as white as the brilliance of the serpent king Ādiśeṣa'—and then continues the genealogy through Arjuna, Malakhāna, and Pratāparudra? Quote the verse that introduces Medinīmalla and name the work and chapter.
rationale: The Bundela royal genealogy beginning with Medinīmalla 'in the lineage of the Sīkāśīrāja' is the patron-dedication unique to this digest; no other Sanskrit smṛti or dharmaśāstra in the corpus carries a 17th-century Orchhā dynastic mangalācaraṇa.

prompt: Which Sanskrit work names its own author's Brahmin family as the 'Dūravāra lineage, a crest-jewel of the glorious Gopācala,' tracing the family from a learned ancestor called Haṃsa, then to a son who 'went in ancient times to the city of the enemy of the cities (Vārāṇasī)' and obtained 'the glorious Caṇḍīśvara, a jewel among Agnihotrins, as his weighty preceptor'? Quote the two verses that name the Dūravāra lineage of Gopācala and the journey to Vārāṇasī under Caṇḍīśvara, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The author's self-identification of his Brahmin family as the Dūravāra clan of Gopācala (Gwalior) and the report that his grandfather studied under Caṇḍīśvara in Vārāṇasī is autobiographical compositional framing unique to this digest; the smṛti texts in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Parāśara, etc.) do not name their authors' families or training-cities.

prompt: Which dharma text repeatedly closes its sections with a colophon naming its compiler as the son of Paraśurāma Miśra and grandson of Haṃsa Paṇḍita, prompted by a king described as 'the sun causing the lotus of the earth encircled by the four oceans to bloom'—the son of Mahārāja Madhukarasāha and grandson of Pratāparudra—and praising the compiler as 'a lion to the great elephant of the world's poverty, the very life of the learned'? Quote one full instance of this colophon and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This signature double-genealogy colophon (compiler's three-generation Brahmin lineage paired with the patron-king's three-generation royal lineage, plus the 'lion to the elephant of the world's poverty' epithet) is the editorial signature of this digest. No other smṛti or dharmaśāstra in the corpus closes its sections with a named patron-and-compiler colophon of this form.
